mappings.rationaleAI: Authority, democracy, freedom, and justice as foundational concepts of the state (A), social contract theory from Hobbes and Locke (A.4.b), popular sovereignty as continuing consent of the governed (A.4.a), the concept of political power (C.1), and human rights as a political concept (C.2). Examination of the limits of legitimate political authority addresses properties of statehood (A.2).

Development of the modern state from ancient Greek citizenship through political theory (A), conceptions of governmental legitimacy including Locke and Marx (A.4), political parties and activism as patterns of political action (B, B.2), and ideology as a driver of political systems (C.3).

Methodological approaches to political ideas from Koselleck through the Cambridge School informing theories of the state (A), historical study of political concepts including legitimacy and governance (A.4), and how intellectual history relates to understanding political ideologies (C.3).

Popular sovereignty and self-governance as consent of the governed (A.4.a), constitutional government and democratic institutions (A.5), human rights in the context of democratic participation (C.2), and liberalism as a political ideology connected to democratic theory (C.3.b.v).

Ideology as a concept in political thought directly addressing the importance of ideology to political systems (C.3.a), covering liberalism, Marxism, and fascism as current political ideologies (C.3.b), and how ideology functions through language and institutions as the relationship between ideological and civil politics (C.3.a).

Human rights as a legal, political, and moral framework directly addressing the concept of human rights (C.2), covering civil and political rights, anti-discrimination protections, and the Universal Declaration of Human Rights, and the state's relationship to individual rights (A.2).

Anarchism as a political philosophy advocating abolition of the state directly corresponding to the anarchism ideology category (C.3.b.i), covering key thinkers from Bakunin through Chomsky as part of modern political ideologies (C.3).
Communist ideology and single-party governance directly corresponding to the communism category (C.3.b.ii), and Communist Party structures as conceptions of political authority and legitimacy (A.4).

Marxism as a political ideology (C.3.b.vi), historical materialism and class struggle connecting to communist theory (C.3.b.ii), and the concept of political power as understood through labor exploitation and critique of political economy (C.1).

Fascism as a political ideology and movement directly corresponding to the fascism category (C.3.b.iv), covering corporatism, Nazism, mass mobilization, and the paradoxical relationship between revolutionary and reactionary impulses.

Liberalism as a political ideology directly corresponding to the liberalism category (C.3.b.v), covering individual freedom, pluralism, justice, and the evolution of liberal thought across European political traditions.

Socialism as a political ideology and movement directly corresponding to the socialism category (C.3.b.viii), covering social democracy, collective ownership, and intersections with feminism and environmentalism.

Nationalism as a political ideology of national self-determination directly corresponding to the nationalism category (C.3.b.vii), and the concept of the nation-state relating to properties of statehood and sovereignty (A.1).

Populism as the political influence of public opinion dividing society into people versus elite (B.4), its function as a thin-centered ideology adaptable to political contexts (C.3), and the relationship between populism and democratic institutions as ideology in political systems (C.3.a).

Neoliberalism as an evolution of liberal economic ideology with commitments to free markets and deregulation (C.3.b.v), and its relationship to nationalist and populist responses as part of the landscape of current political ideologies (C.3.b).

Social contract theory and sovereign authority as the foundational conception of political legitimacy (A.4.b), the state of nature and rational self-interest grounding political obligation (A.4), and the relationship between the state and the individual (A.2).

Defense of liberal values including toleration and responsible government as popular sovereignty (A.4.a), social contract theory grounding political authority (A.4.b), and intellectual origins of constitutional democracy relating to theories of constitutionalism (A.5).

Social contract theory, citizenship, and civil society as political theory foundations (A.4.b), popular sovereignty and direct communion with concepts of liberty and virtue (A.4.a), and the state and the individual through Rousseau's concept of natural human goodness corrupted by institutions (A.2).

Democracy in America as an analysis of popular sovereignty and self-government (A.4.a), constitutional government and democratic institutions (A.5), and adapting traditional political forms to changing conditions through comparative analysis of American democracy and French politics (C.4.d).

On Liberty addressing the state and the individual including individual freedom (A.2), representative government relating to constitutional government theory (A.5), and contributions to liberal political philosophy (C.3.b.v).

Political leadership and the relationship between morality and statecraft addressing conceptions of legitimacy and authority (A.4), and the concept of political power as central to Machiavellian political theory (C.1).
Analysis of totalitarianism relating to fascist ideology (C.3.b.iv), political action and constitutionalism as theory of the state (A, A.5), and concepts of political power and judgment (C.1).

Foundational political theory including ethics and practical philosophy addressing conceptions of the state (A), theories of governance and political organization (A.4), and the concept of political power in the polis (C.1).

Citizenship and political membership relating to the state and the individual (A.2), republican and liberal models as expressions of popular sovereignty (A.4.a), civic participation as political action within communities (B.1), and supranational governance through the EU as a problem of international cooperation (C.4.c).

Freedom of expression as a dimension of the state and the individual relationship (A.2), and rights-based defenses of free speech grounded in Mill's harm principle connecting to the concept of human rights (C.2).

Rule of law as a foundational feature of constitutional government (A.5, A.5.b), and the global diffusion of rule-of-law concepts as adapting traditional political forms across different jurisdictions (C.4.d).

Magna Carta as limiting royal authority representing venerable constitutional sanction (A.4.c), origins of constitutional government (A.5.a), its enduring significance for constitutionalism (A.5), and its role in the development of human rights concepts (C.2).

Federal systems as a form of constitutional government with power-sharing between levels (A.5), and whether federal structures remain viable amid centralization as the problem of adapting traditional political forms (C.4.d).

Authoritarian regimes as conceptions of legitimacy and authority contrasting with democratic governance (A.4), and democratic backsliding as the problem of adapting traditional political forms to changing conditions (C.4.e).

Separation of church and state directly addressing the problem of church and state (C.4.a), and the shift from divine right to secular governance challenging God as a source of political authority (A.4.d).
Cross-religious fundamentalism and its political activism directly relevant to the problem of church and state and the tension between religious authority and secular governance (C.4.a).

Revolutionary overthrow of established authority challenging conceptions of legitimacy (A.4), the concept of political power as contested through revolutionary action (C.1), and revolution as the most dramatic form of adapting political forms to changing conditions (C.4.e).
Terrorism as political action by special-interest groups and movements (B.3), and the use of violence for political aims as an extreme manifestation of the concept of political power (C.1).

Governance theories addressing the state and its institutional frameworks (A), global governance for transnational challenges as international cooperation (C.4.c), and governance reforms as adapting traditional political forms to new institutional demands (C.4.d).
Women's rights and gender equality as a dimension of human rights (C.2), and the feminist movement as political action by a special-interest group advocating for suffrage and social change (B.3).

Toleration as a concept managing the relationship between state and individual across religious and cultural difference (A.2), connections to human rights frameworks (C.2), and its historical role in addressing the problem of church and state (C.4.a).

Utopian thought spanning political theory and its contested relationship to totalitarianism and freedom as ideology (C.3), and utopianism within communism and other political ideologies as current political tendencies (C.3.b).

The national state in the international community through theories of sovereignty and global political order (A.3), and international cooperation and integration as a contemporary political problem (C.4.c).
Covers communicative action theory, discourse ethics, and democratic theory — directly relevant to popular sovereignty and political influence of public opinion.
Covers urban planning tensions, metropolitan growth, and regional consolidation — directly relevant to the urban problem and administration of cities (unresolved leaf C.4.b).
Covers constitutional evolution, parliamentary sovereignty, and constitutional reform — directly relevant to theories of constitutionalism and modern constitutional governments.
Covers the Nazi regime's consolidation of dictatorial power and exploitation of popular fears — directly relevant to fascism as a political ideology.
Covers tensions between national security and individual privacy, mass surveillance, and data protection — relevant to the state and the individual.
